title: Mott transition of photons: quantum Monte Carlo study of Gross-Neveu criticality in a cavity
authors: João C. Inácio, Natanael C. Costa, Fakher F. Assaad
abstract: The Hubbard model on the honeycomb lattice is a pristine realisation of a semimetal-to-insulator Mott transition belonging to the Gross-Neveu O(3) universality class. We couple this system to a single linearly polarised cavity photon mode. The light-matter coupling is such that the photon number remains an intensive quantity as is the case for an empty cavity. For this interacting light-matter model, we formulate a negative-sign-free fermion quantum Monte Carlo algorithm that allows for bias-free results on finite system sizes. Our numerical results show that the coupling to the cavity is irrelevant at criticality, even at strong electron-photon coupling. On the other hand, we observe, and show analytically, that the photon spectral function couples to the optical conductivity of the electronic system. The cavity photons thereby undergo a Mott transition, and the photon spectral function acts as a contact-free non-invasive probe for Mott criticality.
summary: This paper analyzes the coupling between a Mott-insulating electronic system (Hubbard model) and a cavity photon mode. Using advanced quantum simulation techniques, the authors demonstrate that while the coupling does not alter the fundamental quantum critical point, the photon's spectral response becomes directly coupled to the electronic optical conductivity. This establishes the cavity photon as a powerful, non-invasive diagnostic tool for observing metal-to-insulator transitions.
main_problem: The study investigates whether coupling a Mott-transitioning electronic system (Hubbard model) to a cavity photon mode alters the quantum critical point associated with the Mott transition.
main_result: The coupling to the cavity photons is found to be irrelevant at the critical point, but the photon spectral function acts as a non-invasive probe for the electronic Mott transition via its coupling to the optical conductivity.
method: The research employs Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations and advanced field-theoretic techniques, including path integrals and Random Phase Approximation (RPA), to analyze the coupled electron-photon dynamics.
model_or_system: The model consists of the Hubbard model on a honeycomb lattice, representing the electronic system, coupled to a single, linearly polarized cavity photon mode. The coupling is analyzed within the context of Gross-Neveu (GN) criticality.
key_observables: Photon spectral function, electronic spectral function A(K, ω), optical conductivity ($\sigma_{dc}$), and the polariton spectral function.
important_parameters: Interaction strength ($U$), coupling strength ($g$), and the lattice geometry (honeycomb lattice).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the long-wavelength limit for the cavity and that the electron-photon coupling is intensive in the thermodynamic limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the stability of the critical point against coupling strength, the electronic spectral function near the Dirac point, and the emergence/suppression of polariton modes as the system crosses the Mott insulating phase.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the coupled Hamiltonian, then using QMC and field theory to calculate spectral functions, demonstrating that the photon coupling only probes uniform charge fluctuations, and finally concluding that the photon spectrum tracks the Mott transition via polariton formation/disappearance.
why_interesting: This work bridges condensed matter theory (Mott physics, criticality) with quantum optics (cavity QED), showing how cavity modes can serve as sensitive, non-perturbative probes of intrinsic electronic quantum phases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Correlation ratio of the antiferromagnetic order pa- rameter as a function of U for (a) g = 0.5 and (b) g = 4. Inset: data collapse of the AFM correlation ratio with Uc = 3.819(3) and ν = 1.06(2) [37]. We fit the scaling function to a linear form and obtain (a) y = 0.0438(7)x + 0.4174(4) and for (b) y = 0.0454(5)x + 0.4407(4).

FIG. 2. Electronic spectral function at the Dirac point A(K, ω) as a function (a) of g, for L = 12, and (b) of system size, at g = 4. All results are for fixed U = 2 in the DSM phase. The results are obtained through the SAC method on the imaginary-time data G(K, τ) = ⟨ˆc† K(τ)ˆcK(0)⟩.

FIG. 3. Photon function B(ω) as a function (a) of g, for U = 2 in the DSM phase, and (b) of U, for g = 4 across the GN transition. The data shown are for an L = 12 system. B(ω) is obtained through the SAC method on the imaginary- time data D(τ) = ⟨ˆ X(τ) ˆ X(0)⟩.
FIG. 4. Polariton spectral function in the RPA (a) as a func- tion of g at m = 0 and (b) as a function of the mass gap m at g = 4. Here we use β = L = 108, η = 2π/L and Ω= 1.
